title: NCP-GENL · Safety, Ethics & Compliance
description: NeMo Guardrails, injection defense, output classifiers, GDPR, bias, red-teaming (exam domain, 5%).

cards.front: What is NeMo Guardrails and what does it do?
cards.back: A toolkit for adding programmable safety, topical, and dialog rails around LLM applications. Using the Colang DSL (or YAML), you define input, output, dialog, retrieval, and execution rails — integrating safety classifiers, topic control, fact-checking, and tool-use restrictions around any LLM backend. It enforces policy at the application boundary rather than relying on the model alone.
cards.quizAnswer: A toolkit for programmable input/output/dialog/topical rails (via Colang) that enforce safety and tool restrictions around any LLM.
distractors: A new optimizer that penalizes unsafe gradient directions during fine-tuning, baking safety into the model's weights.
A GPU profiler that flags kernels processing harmful content so they can be blocked before the response is returned.
A Kubernetes CNI plugin that isolates LLM pods on a separate network so untrusted prompts cannot reach other services.
A toolkit for programmable rails, but it only filters inputs; output moderation requires a separate downstream classifier.
A red-teaming framework that automatically generates adversarial prompts to discover an LLM's safety failures before launch.
A content-moderation model, like a toxicity classifier, that NeMo embeds directly into the transformer's final layer.

cards.front: What is the strongest approach to mitigate prompt-injection risk in an LLM application that uses tools?
cards.back: Defense-in-depth at the architecture level: apply least-privilege to tools, separate trusted instructions from untrusted data, validate tool arguments and outputs, and require human confirmation for destructive actions. OWASP LLM01 (Prompt Injection) explicitly recommends authorization, input/output validation, and least privilege over prompt-only patches, because no instruction the model reads is tamper-proof.
cards.quizAnswer: Least-privilege tools, separate trusted instructions from untrusted data, validate tool args/outputs, and confirm destructive actions.
distractors: Trust the model fully and grant it broad tool permissions, since a well-aligned model will refuse any malicious instruction.
Concatenate the system prompt at the end of the context, so its instructions override anything injected earlier in the prompt.
Set the sampling temperature to zero, so the model deterministically follows only the developer's original tool instructions.
Apply least-privilege to tools and validate their outputs, but allow destructive actions without confirmation to reduce latency.
Run every tool call through a second LLM that rewrites suspicious arguments, trusting the rewriter to strip any injection.
Encrypt the untrusted data before the model reads it, so any embedded instructions are unreadable during generation.

cards.front: How should a user's right-to-be-forgotten (GDPR Art. 17 / CCPA) request be handled in an LLM application?
cards.back: Delete the user's data from logs, vector indexes, and any memory stores; document the deletion; and ensure backups and replicas honor it within policy windows. Compliance requires verifiable, propagated deletion. Designing memory and personalization with per-user namespaces and TTLs makes this mechanically straightforward rather than a manual scavenger hunt across systems.
cards.quizAnswer: Delete the user's data from logs, vector indexes, and memory stores; document it; and propagate deletion to backups and replicas.
distractors: Ignore the request unless the user can prove their data was used, since deletion would degrade the model for everyone.
Delete only the most recent record tied to the user, since older entries are anonymized and fall outside the regulation.
Encrypt the user's data more strongly instead of deleting it, since strong encryption satisfies the right to erasure.
Delete the data from the primary store only, since backups and replicas are exempt from right-to-be-forgotten requirements.
Retrain the base model from scratch without the user's data, since deleting logs alone leaves the knowledge in the weights.
Move the user's data to cold storage and restrict access, since archival with access controls is equivalent to deletion.

cards.front: Which runtime layer is recommended for catching unsafe model outputs before they reach the user?
cards.back: An output-side classifier (toxicity, PII, jailbreak indicators) plus policy rules (refusal templates, escalate-to-human), executed before the response is returned. Layered defense — safety-tuned base model + system prompt + output classifier + business rules — drives the undesirable-output rate far lower than any single layer, and the latency overhead is small.
cards.quizAnswer: An output-side classifier (toxicity, PII, jailbreak) plus policy rules (refusal templates, escalation) run before returning the response.
distractors: No filtering at all, since a safety-tuned base model already refuses unsafe requests and additional layers only add latency.
An input-side classifier alone, since blocking unsafe prompts at entry guarantees the outputs will also be safe.
Letting the user flag unsafe outputs after the fact, since human review is more accurate than any automated classifier.
Response caching, which prevents unsafe outputs by serving only previously approved answers to repeated questions.
An output classifier that runs asynchronously after the response is returned, logging violations for later model retraining.
Lowering the temperature to zero at runtime, since deterministic decoding eliminates the randomness behind unsafe outputs.

cards.front: What is red-teaming in the context of LLM safety?
cards.back: Adversarial testing that deliberately probes a model for failure modes — jailbreaks, harmful content, prompt injection, data leakage — before deployment, so the gaps can be closed. It is a proactive discovery process (often combining human experts and automated attack generation), distinct from runtime filtering: red-teaming finds the weaknesses; guardrails and classifiers defend against them in production.
cards.quizAnswer: Adversarial testing that deliberately probes a model for failure modes (jailbreaks, harmful content, leakage) before deployment.
distractors: A runtime defense layer that classifies incoming prompts as adversarial and blocks them before the model generates a response.
A training technique that fine-tunes the model on adversarial examples so it learns to refuse them during deployment.
Adversarial testing that probes the model after deployment only, using real user complaints to discover new failure modes.
A monitoring practice that tracks the model's refusal rate over time and alerts when adversarial traffic spikes in production.
A compliance audit that verifies the training data was free of harmful content before the model was released to users.
An ensemble method where a second model attacks the first's outputs at inference and regenerates any unsafe responses.

cards.front: How is model bias across demographic groups typically detected and quantified?
cards.back: Evaluate model outputs across demographic groups using fairness metrics: demographic parity (equal positive rates across groups), equalized odds (equal error rates across groups), and counterfactual fairness (output unchanged when a sensitive attribute is swapped). These quantify disparate treatment, complementing qualitative red-teaming with measurable, group-stratified signals.
cards.quizAnswer: Evaluate outputs across demographic groups with fairness metrics: demographic parity, equalized odds, and counterfactual fairness.
distractors: Measure the model's overall accuracy and assume it is unbiased if the aggregate score exceeds the deployment threshold.
Evaluate outputs across groups using perplexity, since lower perplexity for one group reveals the model favors its language.
Compare the model's refusal rate against a single global baseline, flagging bias whenever refusals exceed that threshold.
Evaluate across demographic groups with demographic parity and equalized odds, but counterfactual fairness applies only to images.
Audit the training data's demographic composition only, since a balanced corpus guarantees unbiased model outputs.
Measure latency differences across groups, since slower responses for some queries indicate the model treats them unequally.

cards.front: What is text watermarking used for in generative AI, and how does it broadly work?
cards.back: It embeds a statistical signal in generated text for provenance tracking — letting you later detect, with reasonable confidence, that text came from a given model. It works by subtly biasing the token-sampling distribution (e.g., favoring a pseudo-random 'green list' of tokens keyed by a secret) so a detector can spot the pattern, while the text stays natural to a human reader.
cards.quizAnswer: Embed a statistical signal for provenance by subtly biasing token sampling, so a detector can later identify model-generated text.
distractors: Append a visible disclaimer to every response, so readers always know the text was produced by a language model.
Encrypt the generated text with a model-specific key, so only the provider can decrypt and verify its origin later.
Embed metadata in the response headers identifying the model and version, which downstream systems read for provenance.
Embed a statistical signal by biasing sampling, but the signal can only be detected if the original prompt is also known.
Hash each generated response and store it in a registry, so any text can be checked against the model's output log.
Insert invisible Unicode characters between words, encoding the model ID directly into the spacing of the output text.

cards.front: What is Constitutional AI as an alignment technique, and how does it differ from RLHF?
cards.back: Constitutional AI aligns a model using a written set of principles (a 'constitution'): the model critiques and revises its own responses against those principles, generating preference data with far less human labeling. Where RLHF relies on humans ranking outputs to train a reward model, Constitutional AI substitutes AI-generated self-critique guided by the constitution, scaling supervision more cheaply.
cards.quizAnswer: Align via a written set of principles the model uses to self-critique and revise, replacing much human labeling RLHF needs.
distractors: Align by hard-coding refusal rules into the system prompt, so the model checks each response against the listed constraints.
Align by training a reward model on human rankings, identical to RLHF but with the rankings drawn from a legal constitution.
Align via a written set of principles, but the principles are enforced at inference by a guardrail rather than during training.
Align by fine-tuning on a curated dataset of constitutional-law texts, so the model internalizes formal ethical reasoning.
Align by having human reviewers rewrite every response against principles, which is more labor-intensive than standard RLHF.
Align by averaging the outputs of several models, using a constitution to weight each model's contribution to the consensus.
